subject: english
chapter_title: Chanda Mama Counts the Stars (Chanda Mama Counts the Stars)

main_sections.title: Chanda Mama Counts the Stars
main_sections.prose_summary: A short, lyrical narrative in which the Moon (Chanda Mama) attempts night after night to count the stars but always loses count and falls asleep. One night, exhausted and in tears, he is consoled by the Sun, who reveals a heartwarming secret: the number of stars in the sky equals the number of lovely children on Earth. Reassured, Chanda Mama stops worrying about counting and instead spends each night contentedly looking at the stars and smiling at the children. Tone: gentle, whimsical, tender. Theme: wonder at the night sky, the comfort of friendship, and the value of children.

reading.section_name: Let us read + Let us think

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us read: [Read the story 'Chanda Mama Counts the Stars' — prose narrative, pp. 109–111.]

Let us think.
A. Answer the following.
1. Does the Moon look the same every night?
2. What did Chanda Mama try to count?
3. Why did Chanda Mama fall asleep?
4. Who helped Chanda Mama?
5. Complete the sentence: The number of stars in the sky is the same as ___________________

question_bank.stem: Does the Moon look the same every night?
What did Chanda Mama try to count?
Why did Chanda Mama fall asleep?
Complete the sentence: The number of stars in the sky is the same as ___.

oracy.section_name: Let us think + Let us listen

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us think.
B. Think and say.
1. I call the Moon, Chanda Mama. What do you call him in your language?
2. Chanda Mama was not able to count the stars. What things can you not count?
3. The Moon is visible during the night. What can you see in the night sky?

question_bank.stem: I call the Moon, Chanda Mama. What do you call him in your language?
Chanda Mama was not able to count the stars. What things can you not count?
The Moon is visible during the night. What can you see in the night sky?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us listen.
A. In the story, we read that the stars were too many to be counted. Now, your teacher will read out a list of items for you. Put the items in the correct column in the given table.
1. Children in the classroom.
2. Flowers in a garden.
3. Books in the library.
4. Pencils in your pencil box.
5. Fingers on your right hand.
6. Children in your city or town.
7. Pages in your English notebook.
8. The hairs on your head.
[Columns: Can be counted easily | Too many to be counted]

question_bank.stem: Listen to your teacher read a list of items. Sort each into the correct column — 'Can be counted easily' or 'Too many to be counted': Children in the classroom; Flowers in a garden; Books in the library; Pencils in your pencil box; Fingers on your right hand; Children in your city or town; Pages in your English notebook; The hairs on your head.

tasks_verbatim.transcript_text: 1. Children in the classroom. 2. Flowers in a garden. 3. Books in the library. 4. Pencils in your pencil box. 5. Fingers on your right hand. 6. Children in your city or town. 7. Pages in your English notebook. 8. The hairs on your head.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us learn.
Here and There!
Look at the following sentences.
We are here on the Earth. The Moon is there in the sky.
I have a sharpener here in my pencil box.
The crayons are there in the big box.
We use 'here' to tell the location of things that are near us. Similarly, 'there' is used to tell the location of things that are far from us.

Fill in the blanks with 'here' or 'there'. One has been done for you.
• The students are playing kabaddi there.
• _______________ is your shirt.
• _______________ is my bicycle.
• I went to Chandigarh last year. My brother lives _______________.
• This is my bench. I sit _______________ with my friends.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blank with 'here' or 'there': ___ is your shirt.
Fill in the blank with 'here' or 'there': ___ is my bicycle.
Fill in the blank with 'here' or 'there': I went to Chandigarh last year. My brother lives ___.
Fill in the blank with 'here' or 'there': This is my bench. I sit ___ with my friends.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us write.
A. It was a full moon night. Children had come out to see the Moon. Complete the conversation they had by picking the words from the help box.
Govind : Wow, look!
Safura : The moon looks so big and bright.
Sukanya : Isn't it?
Dhruv : My grandfather __________________________________.
Ramani : Let us take a picture.
Safura : My mother has ___________________________________.
Dhruv : Come. Stand here and ____________________________.
Govind : The moon is _____________________________________________.
Help Box: a camera for the picture, so bright and big, says the full moon looks very beautiful, let's take a picture

question_bank.stem: Complete the conversation about a full moon night using words from the Help Box: 'a camera for the picture', 'so bright and big', 'says the full moon looks very beautiful', 'let's take a picture'.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Let us do.
Project Work — Wind Chimes
Let us make 'Wind Chimes' with the shapes of the moon and the stars.
Materials required: Two cardboard sheets, coloured paper, a pair of scissors (child-friendly, used under adult supervision), marker, thread, glue.
Steps:
Step 1: Take a cardboard sheet. Cut a crescent-shaped moon from it.
Step 2: Draw stars on cardboard and cut them.
Step 3: Use paper to decorate the sun.
Step 4: Attach all the above shapes using thread and glue to make wind chimes.

question_bank.stem: Make Wind Chimes using cardboard: cut a crescent moon shape, cut out star shapes, decorate with paper, and attach all pieces with thread and glue.
